forward_patch:
--- original
+++ modified
@@ -0,0 +1,55 @@
+---
+assignees:
+- wballard
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: f580
+title: Validator runs as a real agent loop within its per-rule session
+---
+The previous "one session per rule" task (01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543) correctly fixed prompt bleed, but in routing every rule through `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`, it collapsed each rule's session into a single one-shot `prompt()` call. That kills the agentic loop — the validator can no longer use tools to inspect code before deciding.
+
+**Observed in the field (2026-04-25 qwen test run, post-refactor):**
+
+```
+Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning: "<think>
+The user wants me to validate ... I need to check the file ...
+Let me read the file to see what's in it.
+</think>
+
+```json
+{"call_tool":{"arguments":"{\"file_path\": \"...sample_avp_test.rs\"}","name":"read_file"}}
+```"
+```
+
+The model wanted to call `read_file` and `glob` to actually inspect the changed file. The rule prompt itself explicitly invites this: *"You may have access to **files** (read file, glob, grep — read-only) and **code_context** ... Use them to inspect actual code before deciding. Do not guess about file contents."* But because the runner only does one prompt and stops, the tool-call attempt comes back as raw text inside markdown fences, the executor's JSON parser fails, and it silently passes the rule with a warning. Validators are doing exactly what we built them not to do: guessing, then passing without grounding.
+
+**Two things are wrong:**
+
+1. **Single-prompt path.** `avp-common/src/validator/runner.rs::RuleTask::execute` (line ~495) calls `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`, which is hard-coded to `initialize` → `new_session` → one `prompt()` → collect. There is no place for the runner to react to a tool-call attempt, an unparseable response, or a request from the model to continue. **One session per rule does not mean one turn per rule.**
+
+2. **Tools may not even be wired.** `avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_tools` (around line 316) returns `(None, None)` — i.e. no MCP, no tool registry — unless `SAH_HTTP_PORT` / `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT` is set in the env. That comment explicitly says "the validator runs without tools (prompt-only, backward compatible)." That's the upstream reason qwen's tool-call attempt landed as raw text: there were no real tools registered, so even if llama-agent had recognized the format, there'd be nothing to execute. The validator prompts advertise tools that may not actually be available.
+
+**What "doing a better job" means here (do NOT revert per-rule isolation — that part is correct):**
+
+- Keep one fresh agent session per rule (that's the prompt-bleed fix from 01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543).
+- Within each per-rule session, run a real agent loop. Concretely: replace the one-shot `execute_prompt_with_agent` call in `RuleTask::execute` with a runner-side loop that:
+  1. Sends the rule prompt on a fresh session.
+  2. Inspects the response. If it's valid pass/fail JSON, accept and finish.
+  3. If the response indicates a tool-call attempt (either via the agent's structured tool-call path OR by the model emitting JSON like `{"call_tool": ...}` because the agent's tool registry isn't recognizing the format), execute the tool and continue the same session with the result.
+  4. If the response is non-JSON narrative ("Let me read the file..."), send a follow-up prompt on the same session — either "execute these tools and continue" or, as a fallback, "respond with the required JSON only."
+  5. Loop until we get parseable JSON or hit a turn cap (e.g. 6 turns) — at which point fail the rule with a clear message rather than passing it on a warning.
+- Make tools actually available. Audit `resolve_validator_tools` in `context.rs` — the `(None, None)` "backward compatible" path is the wrong default for a code-quality validator that's instructed to read files. Either always wire up the read-only file tools (Read/Glob/Grep) for validators, or make it configurable per-RuleSet via the `VALIDATOR.md` manifest. Validators that ask for tools must get tools.
+- Fix the parse-fail-then-pass behavior: a validator that returns unparseable text should NOT silently pass. The current `executor.rs` "Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning" path produces false greens. Switch the policy to: unparseable → fail, with the raw text and stop_reason in the failure message — at least until the agent loop is robust.
+
+**Why:** A validator that "passes" because the model couldn't get its tool call recognized is worse than no validator at all — it gives false confidence. The whole point of separating rules into their own sessions was to make their judgments independent and trustworthy. We have to also let them be agentic.
+
+**How to apply:**
+1. Add a runner-side multi-turn helper. Reuse the agent's existing `prompt()` semantics (the agent already runs an internal agentic loop on each prompt — this is about *our* loop on top of that). Each iteration after the first sends a `PromptRequest` on the same `session_id` returned by the first `new_session` call for that rule. Subscribe to notifications once and keep the receiver alive for the whole session.
+2. Decide what counts as a "tool-call attempt" outside the agent's normal channel and what to do. Two reasonable strategies, in increasing order of effort: (a) on any non-JSON response, send one follow-up nudge prompt that includes the prior assistant text and asks for JSON only; (b) detect inline `{"call_tool": ...}` patterns and synthesize a tool execution server-side. Start with (a); revisit (b) if (a) doesn't get qwen to converge.
+3. Wire tools. Track down `resolve_validator_tools` and produce a default validator tool set (file read + code_context read paths) without requiring `SAH_HTTP_PORT`. Match what the rule prompts advertise.
+4. Change the parse-fail policy in `avp-common/src/validator/executor.rs` from "pass with warning" to "fail with the raw text and a clear message about what went wrong." Add a regression test for it.
+5. Add a regression test for the agent-loop path: construct a fake agent that returns non-JSON on turn 1 and valid JSON on turn 2; assert the rule converges and the result is the turn-2 verdict, in the same session_id.
+6. Re-run the manual qwen Stop-hook test (write a deliberately-bad Rust file, stop) and confirm: (a) at least one rule actually executes a tool to read the file, (b) `code-quality` returns specific findings (magic numbers, hardcoded IPs, hardcoded `return 42`), and (c) no `Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning` lines appear in `.avp/log`.
+
+**Trade-off accepted:** higher latency per validator (multiple turns / tool calls per rule) in exchange for actually using the model's reasoning + tool use to ground each verdict. Keep the per-RuleSet concurrency limiter so total wall time doesn't explode.
+
+**Cross-reference:** This task supersedes nothing — 01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543's per-rule isolation must remain intact. This is the second half of that work that the original task description didn't fully account for.
\ No newline at end of file

 ---
-The previous "one session per rule" task (01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543) correctly fixed prompt bleed, but in routing every rule through `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`, it collapsed each rule's session into a single one-shot `prompt()` call. That kills the agentic loop — the validator can no longer use tools to inspect code before deciding.
+**Order from user (2026-04-25):** "Revert our attempt to have one validator per session and do a better job — one validator per session does not mean 'not an agent.'" Followed by "go until it stops" and "I'm OK with a large turn limit like 256 just to prevent runaway."

+The previous "one session per rule" task (01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543) introduced the bug. That task replaced the existing per-rule path with `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`, a one-shot helper that does `initialize` → `new_session` → one `prompt()` → collect → done. The intent of that task was prompt isolation. The unintended consequence was the death of agentic behavior: the validator can no longer use tools, can no longer recover from a non-JSON response, can no longer continue a thought.
+
 **Observed in the field (2026-04-25 qwen test run, post-refactor):**

 ```
@@ -20,36 +22,49 @@
 ```"
 ```

-The model wanted to call `read_file` and `glob` to actually inspect the changed file. The rule prompt itself explicitly invites this: *"You may have access to **files** (read file, glob, grep — read-only) and **code_context** ... Use them to inspect actual code before deciding. Do not guess about file contents."* But because the runner only does one prompt and stops, the tool-call attempt comes back as raw text inside markdown fences, the executor's JSON parser fails, and it silently passes the rule with a warning. Validators are doing exactly what we built them not to do: guessing, then passing without grounding.

-**Two things are wrong:**
+---

-1. **Single-prompt path.** `avp-common/src/validator/runner.rs::RuleTask::execute` (line ~495) calls `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`, which is hard-coded to `initialize` → `new_session` → one `prompt()` → collect. There is no place for the runner to react to a tool-call attempt, an unparseable response, or a request from the model to continue. **One session per rule does not mean one turn per rule.**
+## Step 1 (mandatory, do first): revert the one-shot path

-2. **Tools may not even be wired.** `avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_tools` (around line 316) returns `(None, None)` — i.e. no MCP, no tool registry — unless `SAH_HTTP_PORT` / `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT` is set in the env. That comment explicitly says "the validator runs without tools (prompt-only, backward compatible)." That's the upstream reason qwen's tool-call attempt landed as raw text: there were no real tools registered, so even if llama-agent had recognized the format, there'd be nothing to execute. The validator prompts advertise tools that may not actually be available.
+Specifically:
+- In `avp-common/src/validator/runner.rs::RuleTask::execute` (currently around line 490–510), the call to `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent(...)` is the bug. **Remove that call.** Restore the pre-refactor pattern of doing `initialize` → `new_session` → and then driving `prompt()` directly on that session, so the runner — not a single helper call — owns the conversation.
+- Keep the per-rule fresh session (i.e., do not revert all the way to the old shared-session model from before 01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543). Per-rule isolation is correct and must remain.
+- Net effect: each rule still gets its own session (no prompt bleed), but the runner now controls how many `prompt()` turns happen on that session, can react to tool calls, and can collect notifications across multiple turns.

-**What "doing a better job" means here (do NOT revert per-rule isolation — that part is correct):**
+The revert is the floor, not the ceiling. Steps 2–5 below are how we then "do a better job."

-- Keep one fresh agent session per rule (that's the prompt-bleed fix from 01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543).
-- Within each per-rule session, run a real agent loop. Concretely: replace the one-shot `execute_prompt_with_agent` call in `RuleTask::execute` with a runner-side loop that:
-  1. Sends the rule prompt on a fresh session.
-  2. Inspects the response. If it's valid pass/fail JSON, accept and finish.
-  3. If the response indicates a tool-call attempt (either via the agent's structured tool-call path OR by the model emitting JSON like `{"call_tool": ...}` because the agent's tool registry isn't recognizing the format), execute the tool and continue the same session with the result.

+title: Revert validator one-shot prompt change; restore agent loop with per-rule sessions
 ---
-Supersedes 01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543 (whose implementation was reverted on 2026-04-25 because it killed the agent loop by routing through `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`, a one-shot helper).
+**Order from user (2026-04-25):** "Revert our attempt to have one validator per session and do a better job — one validator per session does not mean 'not an agent.'" Followed by "go until it stops" and "I'm OK with a large turn limit like 256 just to prevent runaway."

-This task captures the corrected approach **on top of the restored prior code** (now back in the working tree).
+The previous "one session per rule" task (01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543) introduced the bug. That task replaced the existing per-rule path with `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`, a one-shot helper that does `initialize` → `new_session` → one `prompt()` → collect → done. The intent of that task was prompt isolation. The unintended consequence was the death of agentic behavior: the validator can no longer use tools, can no longer recover from a non-JSON response, can no longer continue a thought.

-## Where the prior code stands (post-revert, what's actually in `avp-common/src/validator/runner.rs::execute_ruleset` right now)
+**Observed in the field (2026-04-25 qwen test run, post-refactor):**

-- One agent session per RuleSet: `new_session` is called once before the rule loop (around line 866).
-- An init prompt is sent on that session carrying the hook context / changed files (around line 908).
-- The rule loop (line 938) iterates `for rule in &ruleset.rules` and sends one `agent.prompt()` per rule on the **same** `session_id` (line 955).
-- Each `agent.prompt()` runs the agent's internal agentic loop — so tool calls within a rule (if tools are wired and the format is recognized) ARE multi-turn. This is the property we must preserve.
+```
+Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning: "<think>
+The user wants me to validate ... I need to check the file ...
+Let me read the file to see what's in it.
+</think>

-## The single defect to fix here
+```json
+{"call_tool":{"arguments":"{\"file_path\": \"...sample_avp_test.rs\"}","name":"read_file"}}
+```"
+```
+
+The model wanted to call `read_file` to actually inspect the changed file — exactly what the rule prompt invites: *"You may have access to **files** ... Use them to inspect actual code before deciding. Do not guess about file contents."* But because the runner only does one prompt and stops, the tool-call attempt comes back as raw text, the executor's JSON parser fails, and it silently passes the rule with a warning. Validators are doing exactly what we built them not to do: guessing, then passing without grounding.
+
+---
+
+## Step 1 (mandatory, do first): revert the one-shot path

-Rules within a RuleSet share `session_id`, so rule N sees rule N-1's prompt + response in its conversation history → prompt bleed. Observed in the field on 2026-04-25 morning: `security-rules:no-secrets` returned text framed entirely around `input-validation` concerns ("no SQL injection, XSS, command injection, path traversal, XXE, or deserialization issues"), having absorbed the prior rule's vocabulary.
+Specifically:
+- In `avp-common/src/validator/runner.rs::RuleTask::execute` (currently around line 490–510), the call to `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent(...)` is the bug. **Remove that call.** Restore the pre-refactor pattern of doing `initialize` → `new_session` → and then driving `prompt()` directly on that session, so the runner — not a single helper call — owns the conversation.
+- Keep the per-rule fresh session (i.e., do not revert all the way to the old shared-session model from before 01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543). Per-rule isolation is correct and must remain.
+- Net effect: each rule still gets its own session (no prompt bleed), but the runner now controls how many `prompt()` turns happen on that session, can react to tool calls, and can collect notifications across multiple turns.
+
+The revert is the floor, not the ceiling. Steps 2–5 below are how we then "do a better job."
+
+## Step 2: real agent loop within the per-rule session
+
+Within each per-rule session, run a real loop in the runner:
+1. Send the rule prompt on the fresh session.
+2. Inspect the response. If it's valid pass/fail JSON, accept and finish.
+3. If the response indicates a tool-call attempt (via the agent's structured tool-call path, OR via the model emitting JSON like `{"call_tool": ...}` because the agent's tool registry didn't recognize the format), execute the tool and continue the same session with the result.
+4. If the response is non-JSON narrative ("Let me read the file..."), send a follow-up prompt on the same session — either "execute these tools and continue" or, as a fallback, "respond with the required JSON only."
+5. Loop until the agent converges on parseable JSON on its own. **Runaway-only ceiling: 256 turns** — this is a safety net for a misbehaving model, not a budget the loop is supposed to spend. Typical convergence should be 1–3 turns. If a rule actually hits 256, fail it with a clear "ran away" message and surface enough context to investigate.

-## The surgical change
+## Step 3: wire tools so the agent can actually use them

-Move `new_session` (and any necessary init context) **inside** the rule loop. That's it.
+`avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_tools` (around line 316) currently returns `(None, None)` — i.e. no MCP, no tool registry — unless `SAH_HTTP_PORT` / `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT` is set in the env. The comment says "the validator runs without tools (prompt-only, backward compatible)." That's the upstream reason qwen's tool-call attempt landed as raw text: there were no real tools registered, so even if the format had been recognized, there'd be nothing to execute. Either always wire up the read-only file/code_context tools for validators, or make it configurable per-RuleSet via the `VALIDATOR.md` manifest. Validators that ask for tools must get tools.

-Concretely, in `runner.rs::execute_ruleset`:
-- Hoist nothing about the agent.prompt loop. Keep `agent.prompt(rule_request)` exactly as-is. Do NOT introduce `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`. Do NOT add a runner-side multi-turn loop. The agent's internal loop already handles tool use across multiple turns within a single `prompt()` call.
-- Move the `new_session` call (and the `cwd`/`NewSessionRequest` setup) from before the rule loop into the loop body, so each rule starts with a fresh session_id.
-- Remove (or fold into the per-rule prompt) the separate "init prompt" turn. Each rule's prompt should be self-contained: hook context, changed files, the rule body, response-format instructions. The init-prompt-then-rule-prompt pattern was a way to amortize context across rules in a shared session — once we're not sharing, it costs more than it saves and is one more channel for state to leak across rules. The rule prompt template at `builtin/prompts/.system/rule.md` may already include the hook context; verify before changing.
-- Keep the per-rule notification subscription (`self.notifier.subscribe_session(&session_id.0)`) and the spawned collector inside the loop (already there).
-- Keep the `concurrency.report_success` / rate-limit handling (already there).
+## Step 4: stop silently passing on parse failure

-Optional follow-up (only if measurements show wall-time pain): run rules within a RuleSet in parallel via `FuturesUnordered` bounded by the existing `ConcurrencyLimiter`. Not required for this task — sequential per-rule fresh-session is fine and is the smallest correct change.
+`avp-common/src/validator/executor.rs` currently logs "Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning" and treats the rule as passed. That produces false greens. Switch the policy: unparseable → fail, with the raw text and stop_reason in the failure message. Once the agent loop in step 2 is solid, parse failures should be rare; when they happen, they should be loud.

-## Acceptance
+## Tests required

-- A RuleSet with two rules whose prompts would interfere if shared (e.g. one frames itself as "input-validation" and the next is `no-secrets`) produces independent verdicts. The qwen 2026-04-25 repro must not happen on a re-run.
-- The agent loop within each rule is preserved: a model that wants to call a tool can still do so via the agent's normal channel and the tool result is fed back into the same `prompt()` call's internal loop.
-- `cargo test -p avp-common` and `cargo clippy -p avp-common --all-targets -- -D warnings` are clean.
-- A regression test asserts each rule in a RuleSet sees a distinct `session_id` (the `test_execute_ruleset_uses_fresh_session_per_rule` style of test from the reverted attempt was correct — recover that test pattern, point it at the corrected implementation).
+- Regression test for agent-loop path: construct a fake agent that returns non-JSON on turn 1 and valid JSON on turn 2; assert the rule converges on the turn-2 verdict, on the same session_id, without hitting the runaway ceiling.
+- Regression test that 01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543's isolation is preserved: two rules in the same RuleSet must still see distinct session_ids (the existing `test_execute_ruleset_uses_fresh_session_per_rule` should keep passing).
+- Regression test for the parse-fail policy change: unparseable response → rule fails (not passes-with-warning).
+- Manual qwen Stop-hook re-test: write a deliberately-bad Rust file (magic numbers, hardcoded IPs, `return 42`), stop, and confirm: (a) at least one rule actually executes a tool to read the file, (b) `code-quality` returns specific findings, (c) no `Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning` lines appear in `.avp/log`.

-## Out of scope (separate tasks — see notes below)
+## What this task is NOT

-- Validator tool wiring. `avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_tools` returns `(None, None)` unless `SAH_HTTP_PORT` / `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT` is set, so validators currently run with no tool registry by default. This is why qwen's `read_file` attempt landed as raw `{"call_tool": ...}` text in the response. **Important upstream bug**, but it predates the per-rule-session work and is independent of it. File as its own task.
-- Unparseable → silent pass. `avp-common/src/validator/executor.rs` currently logs `"Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning"` and treats the rule as passed, producing false greens. Should be a fail with the raw text and stop_reason. **Independent of session topology**, file as its own task.
+- NOT a revert of the prompt-bleed fix (01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543). Per-rule fresh sessions stay.
+- NOT introducing a turn-cap that's a meaningful budget. 256 is a runaway tripwire, full stop.
+- NOT optional. The current state ships a validator that silently passes when the model wants to use tools — that's worse than no validator.

-## Why the small scope
+## Why

-The prior attempt at this task (01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543) over-engineered: replaced the per-rule call site with a one-shot helper, added a turn-loop in the runner, restructured prompts. That broke the agent loop. The actual problem — shared `session_id` across rules — needs only a session being created in a different place. Keep the surgery to the smallest change that fixes the observed bug.
\ No newline at end of file
+A validator that "passes" because the model couldn't get its tool call recognized is worse than no validator at all — it gives false confidence. The whole point of separating rules into their own sessions was to make their judgments independent and trustworthy. We have to also let them be agentic.
\ No newline at end of file

Either always wire up the read-only file/code_context tools for validators, or make it configurable per-RuleSet via the `VALIDATOR.md` manifest. Validators that ask for tools must get tools.

-Concretely, in `runner.rs::execute_ruleset`:
-- Hoist nothing about the agent.prompt loop. Keep `agent.prompt(rule_request)` exactly as-is. Do NOT introduce `claude_agent::execute_prompt_with_agent`. Do NOT add a runner-side multi-turn loop. The agent's internal loop already handles tool use across multiple turns within a single `prompt()` call.
-- Move the `new_session` call (and the `cwd`/`NewSessionRequest` setup) from before the rule loop into the loop body, so each rule starts with a fresh session_id.
-- Remove (or fold into the per-rule prompt) the separate "init prompt" turn. Each rule's prompt should be self-contained: hook context, changed files, the rule body, response-format instructions. The init-prompt-then-rule-prompt pattern was a way to amortize context across rules in a shared session — once we're not sharing, it costs more than it saves and is one more channel for state to leak across rules. The rule prompt template at `builtin/prompts/.system/rule.md` may already include the hook context; verify before changing.
-- Keep the per-rule notification subscription (`self.notifier.subscribe_session(&session_id.0)`) and the spawned collector inside the loop (already there).
-- Keep the `concurrency.report_success` / rate-limit handling (already there).
+## Step 4: stop silently passing on parse failure

-Optional follow-up (only if measurements show wall-time pain): run rules within a RuleSet in parallel via `FuturesUnordered` bounded by the existing `ConcurrencyLimiter`. Not required for this task — sequential per-rule fresh-session is fine and is the smallest correct change.
+`avp-common/src/validator/executor.rs` currently logs "Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning" and treats the rule as passed. That produces false greens. Switch the policy: unparseable → fail, with the raw text and stop_reason in the failure message. Once the agent loop in step 2 is solid, parse failures should be rare; when they happen, they should be loud.

-## Acceptance
+## Tests required

-- A RuleSet with two rules whose prompts would interfere if shared (e.g. one frames itself as "input-validation" and the next is `no-secrets`) produces independent verdicts. The qwen 2026-04-25 repro must not happen on a re-run.
-- The agent loop within each rule is preserved: a model that wants to call a tool can still do so via the agent's normal channel and the tool result is fed back into the same `prompt()` call's internal loop.
-- `cargo test -p avp-common` and `cargo clippy -p avp-common --all-targets -- -D warnings` are clean.
-- A regression test asserts each rule in a RuleSet sees a distinct `session_id` (the `test_execute_ruleset_uses_fresh_session_per_rule` style of test from the reverted attempt was correct — recover that test pattern, point it at the corrected implementation).
+- Regression test for agent-loop path: construct a fake agent that returns non-JSON on turn 1 and valid JSON on turn 2; assert the rule converges on the turn-2 verdict, on the same session_id, without hitting the runaway ceiling.
+- Regression test that 01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543's isolation is preserved: two rules in the same RuleSet must still see distinct session_ids (the existing `test_execute_ruleset_uses_fresh_session_per_rule` should keep passing).
+- Regression test for the parse-fail policy change: unparseable response → rule fails (not passes-with-warning).
+- Manual qwen Stop-hook re-test: write a deliberately-bad Rust file (magic numbers, hardcoded IPs, `return 42`), stop, and confirm: (a) at least one rule actually executes a tool to read the file, (b) `code-quality` returns specific findings, (c) no `Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning` lines appear in `.avp/log`.

-## Out of scope (separate tasks — see notes below)
+## What this task is NOT

-- Validator tool wiring. `avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_tools` returns `(None, None)` unless `SAH_HTTP_PORT` / `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT` is set, so validators currently run with no tool registry by default. This is why qwen's `read_file` attempt landed as raw `{"call_tool": ...}` text in the response. **Important upstream bug**, but it predates the per-rule-session work and is independent of it. File as its own task.
-- Unparseable → silent pass. `avp-common/src/validator/executor.rs` currently logs `"Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning"` and treats the rule as passed, producing false greens. Should be a fail with the raw text and stop_reason. **Independent of session topology**, file as its own task.
+- NOT a revert of the prompt-bleed fix (01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543). Per-rule fresh sessions stay.
+- NOT introducing a turn-cap that's a meaningful budget. 256 is a runaway tripwire, full stop.
+- NOT optional. The current state ships a validator that silently passes when the model wants to use tools — that's worse than no validator.

-## Why the small scope
+## Why

-The prior attempt at this task (01KQ2HZPTX6EQVZ6YFAYZGY543) over-engineered: replaced the per-rule call site with a one-shot helper, added a turn-loop in the runner, restructured prompts. That broke the agent loop. The actual problem — shared `session_id` across rules — needs only a session being created in a different place. Keep the surgery to the smallest change that fixes the observed bug.
\ No newline at end of file
+A validator that "passes" because the model couldn't get its tool call recognized is worse than no validator at all — it gives false confidence. The whole point of separating rules into their own sessions was to make their judgments independent and trustworthy. We have to also let them be agentic.
\ No newline at end of file
